message:
feat(web,scripts,manifest): expand complete guide content and harden installer scripts

apps/web — Complete Guide content additions:
- Add best practices guides link and advice to keep them up to date
- Add guidance on when to stop refining plans and transition to beads
- Add plan-bead gap warning (agents getting stuck between plan and bead creation)
- Add warning against writing pseudo-beads in markdown documents
- Add worktree avoidance rationale with Agent Mail as the alternative
- Add "no ringleader" principle for swarm coordination
- Add "First 10 Minutes After Launch" numbered walkthrough section

scripts/lib/update.sh — Codex CLI install hardening:
- Replace run_cmd_bun_with_retry cascade with unified retry loop across
  @latest, unversioned, and pinned fallback packages (3 attempts each)
- Add dry-run support for Codex install
- Fix MCP Agent Mail failure output to use cursor-up/overwrite pattern
  consistent with the rest of the installer
- Remove CAUT (coding_agent_usage_tracker) install block — tool is no
  longer maintained
- Harden update_root_agents_md generator search with explicit array
  and ACFS_REPO_ROOT guard

scripts/services-setup.sh:
- Fix variable scoping: remove accidental local re-declaration of
  selected_lines inside select_dcg_packs

packages/manifest/src/generate.ts:
- Handle empty string edge case in shellQuoteVerifiedInstallerArg

scripts/generated/internal_checksums.sh:
- Update SHA256 for update.sh to match new content

beads: update issue metadata

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

@@ -617,6 +617,7 @@ {"id":"bd-eel0","title":"Harden curl|bash temp file handling (mktemp; avoid /tmp script execution)","description":"install.sh downloads helper libs (context/state/report/ubuntu_upgrade) into predictable /tmp filenames (e.g., /tmp/acfs-context-14822.sh) and run_preflight_checks may execute /tmp/acfs-preflight.sh if present. This creates a symlink/race attack surface (and can run attacker-controlled /tmp scripts). Fix by using mktemp for all downloaded helper scripts and never trusting pre-existing /tmp scripts; keep behavior identical otherwise.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2025-12-25T07:21:36.723024Z","updated_at":"2025-12-25T07:33:31.746204Z","closed_at":"2025-12-25T07:33:31.746204Z","close_reason":"Completed: use mktemp for downloaded helper scripts/tarballs and never execute /tmp preflight (commit 3c22af8)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0} {"id":"bd-egf5","title":"TASK: Update /wizard/ssh-connect for password-first connection","description":"## Parent Feature agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-t66c (Wizard Password-First Flow Updates) ## Summary Update the SSH Connect wizard page to use password-based connection command instead of key-based. Users will SSH as root with password for their first connection. ## Current State Primary command shown: \`ssh -i ~/.ssh/acfs_ed25519 ubuntu@IP\` This fails if the user didn't successfully add their SSH key to the provider. ## Target State Primary command: \`ssh root@IP\` With clear expectation that password will be requested. ## Implementation Details ### File: apps/web/app/wizard/ssh-connect/page.tsx ### Key Changes 1. **Update primary command** \`\`\`typescript const sshCommand = \`ssh root@\${vpsIP}\`; // Remove the -i flag and key path // Remove ubuntu user, use root \`\`\` 2. **Add password expectation** \`\`\`tsx When you run this command, it will ask for your password. This is the root password from your VPS provider. \`\`\` 3. **Update success indicator** Change expected prompt from \`ubuntu@vps:~$\` to \`root@vps:~#\` 4. **Update SimplerGuide** - Add step: "Enter your password when prompted" - Explain: "Nothing will appear as you type - that's normal" - Clarify: "This is the last time you'll need the password" 5. **Update troubleshooting** - "Wrong password" - check VPS provider email/panel - "Connection refused" - VPS still booting, wait 2 min - Remove SSH key specific errors from primary list ### New Password Guidance \`\`\`tsx When prompted, type your root password and press Enter.

Important: As you type, nothing will appear on screen. This is a security feature, not a bug! Just type your password and press Enter. \`\`\` ### Fallback Section Keep but reframe: > **Ubuntu user instead of root?** > Some providers use 'ubuntu' as the default user. If root doesn't work, try: > \`ssh ubuntu@IP\` > You may still need a password, or the provider might have set up a key. ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Primary command is password-based (no -i flag) - [ ] Uses root user by default - [ ] Password expectation clearly stated - [ ] "Nothing appears when typing" explained - [ ] Expected prompt shows root@ - [ ] Troubleshooting updated for password flow - [ ] SimplerGuide steps updated - [ ] Mobile layout verified ## Testing 1. Follow instructions with fresh password-only VPS 2. Verify command works 3. Verify password prompt appears as described 4. Check troubleshooting covers common password issues ## Dependencies - TASK: Update /wizard/create-vps (should come first so flow is coherent) ## Files Modified - apps/web/app/wizard/ssh-connect/page.tsx","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T00:20:22.454663Z","updated_at":"2025-12-22T00:35:05.365902Z","closed_at":"2025-12-22T00:35:05.365902Z","close_reason":"Completed (page already matches password-first flow)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-egf5","depends_on_id":"bd-5yty","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-22T00:20:50.121846Z","created_by":"daemon","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]} {"id":"bd-eh383","title":"meta_skill ARM64 Linux binary missing — blocked upstream (GH #115)","description":"## Context GitHub issue #115 filed by tayiorbeii (2026-02-06). On ARM64 Linux (aarch64), meta_skill install 404s because v0.1.0 has no aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu binary. ## Available binaries in v0.1.0: - ms-0.1.0-aarch64-apple-darwin.tar.gz (ARM64 macOS - exists) - ms-0.1.0-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu.tar.gz (x86_64 Linux - exists) - ms-0.1.0-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc.zip (Windows - exists) - aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu — MISSING ## Two-Part Fix ### Part 1: ACFS workaround (THIS repo, can do now) Update doctor.sh check at lines 1115-1116 to detect aarch64 on Linux and show: "meta_skill: ARM64 Linux binary not yet available (see https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/meta_skill/issues/1)" instead of the generic install suggestion that will 404. Implementation: ```bash # In doctor.sh, before the meta_skill install suggestion: if [[ "$(uname -m)" == "aarch64" ]] && [[ "$(uname -s)" == "Linux" ]]; then log_item "warn" "meta_skill (ms)" "ARM64 Linux binary not yet available" return 0 # Skip install attempt fi ``` ### Part 2: Upstream (meta_skill repo, blocked) Add aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu to the GoReleaser or release CI matrix in Dicklesworthstone/meta_skill. Requires: - Cross-compilation target or dedicated ARM64 runner - New release (v0.1.1 or v0.2.0) This is blocked on upstream release work. ## Testing After Part 1: 1. On this VPS (x86_64): `acfs doctor` should still show meta_skill normally 2. Simulate ARM64: mock uname output or grep the code to verify the aarch64 check exists 3. Verify the issue URL is correct and accessible ## Key Files - scripts/lib/doctor.sh (lines 1109-1117, meta_skill check) - Upstream: Dicklesworthstone/meta_skill release CI matrix ## Status Part 1 is actionable now. Part 2 is blocked upstream. Keep this bead open until both parts are done.","notes":"ACFS-side mitigation is complete: Linux ARM64 now warns correctly in doctor.sh, builds meta_skill from source in the generated installer, scripts/lib/update.sh, and scripts/lib/doctor_fix.sh, and now has regression coverage in tests/unit/test_doctor_generated.sh plus manifest and doctor-fix/update tests. Remaining work is upstream binary publishing for aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu in meta_skill.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-02-08T18:16:39.015874050Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-13T00:54:56Z","closed_at":"2026-03-13T00:54:56Z","close_reason":"Closed as locally complete: ACFS now avoids the broken ARM64 Linux installer path and has regression coverage; remaining work belongs upstream in Dicklesworthstone/meta_skill issue #1.","external_ref":"gh:Dicklesworthstone/agentic_coding_flywheel_setup#115","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["arm64","bug","meta_skill","upstream-blocked"],"comments":[{"id":83,"issue_id":"bd-eh383","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Part 1 complete: doctor.sh updated to detect ARM64 Linux and show a helpful warning + build-from-source suggestion instead of the install script that would 404. Part 2 (upstream meta_skill binary publish) still pending.","created_at":"2026-02-11T17:11:27Z"}]} +{"id":"bd-eke1f","title":"Deep cross-agent audit of current shared edits","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-14T03:34:31.227273730Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-14T03:34:31.227273730Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0} {"id":"bd-eli","title":"Research CASS session data format and API","description":"# Task: Research CASS session data format and API ## Context Part of EPIC: Agent Session Sharing and Replay (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-0sb) ## Why This Comes First Before we can export sessions, we need to understand how CASS stores them: - What format is the session data? - What commands does CASS expose? - What fields are available for export? - Are there multiple session types (Claude vs Codex vs Gemini)? ## Research Questions 1. Run 'cass --help' and document available commands 2. Run 'cass list' and examine output format 3. Run 'cass get --json' and examine structure 4. Check if CASS stores sessions per-agent or unified 5. Identify what data is NOT stored (we can't export what doesn't exist) ## Deliverables - Document CASS CLI commands and their output - Example session data structure - List of exportable fields - Any limitations or gaps discovered ## Acceptance Criteria - CASS API documented - Session format understood - Export schema can be designed based on actual data - Gaps identified for follow-up ## Notes This is research, not implementation. Output is documentation that informs subsequent tasks.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T17:51:13.705596Z","updated_at":"2025-12-21T20:52:01.467990Z","closed_at":"2025-12-21T20:52:01.467990Z","close_reason":"Research complete. Documented CASS session format, CLI commands, search/export APIs, session storage locations, and identified 7 limitations. Created docs/cass-session-format-research.md.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0} {"id":"bd-eooz","title":"Add password clarification (VPS root vs provider account)","description":"# Task: Add Password Clarification ## Parent Epic [EPIC] First SSH Connection Experience (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-q7r3) ## Description Users frequently confuse their VPS provider account password (Hetzner/Contabo login) with the VPS root password (set during VPS creation). This is the #1 support issue. They try the wrong password, get locked out, and panic. ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/app/wizard/ssh-connect/page.tsx Add a PROMINENT AlertCard above the SSH command: \`\`\`tsx

When SSH asks for a password, use the VPS root password — the one you created (or received via email) when setting up the VPS.

❌ NOT this password:

Your Hetzner/Contabo/OVH account login

✓ USE this password:

The root password from VPS creation

Some providers email you the root password. Check your inbox if you didn't set one.

\`\`\` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Appears PROMINENTLY, not buried in SimplerGuide - [ ] Clear visual distinction between wrong and right password - [ ] Mentions email option for some providers - [ ] Uses warning styling to grab attention - [ ] Works well on mobile (stacked layout) ## UI/UX Notes - This is SO common that it deserves main-content placement - The two-column comparison makes it instantly clear - Mobile should stack vertically - Consider adding link back to VPS creation step if they forgot","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T18:41:38.986261Z","updated_at":"2025-12-22T19:02:19.783445Z","closed_at":"2025-12-22T19:02:19.783445Z","close_reason":"Already covered: ssh-connect/page.tsx has AlertCard + Step 6 explaining VPS password vs hidden typing","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0} {"id":"bd-epc","title":"[codex.1] Fix doctor.sh codex auth detection","description":"## Goal Update scripts/lib/doctor.sh to correctly detect codex-cli authentication via OAuth tokens instead of checking for OPENAI_API_KEY. ## Current (Incorrect) Behavior The check_codex_auth() function currently: 1. Checks for OPENAI_API_KEY environment variable 2. Looks for OPENAI_API_KEY in ~/.zshrc.local 3. Looks for OPENAI_API_KEY in /data/projects/.envrc 4. Reports "pass" if any of these exist ## Correct Behavior Should instead: 1. Check for ~/.codex/auth.json (or similar OAuth token file) 2. Optionally verify the token isn't expired 3. Report "pass" if OAuth credentials exist 4. Suggest running "codex auth" if not authenticated ## Technical Notes - Need to verify the actual path where codex-cli stores OAuth tokens - The token file location may vary by OS/version - Common locations: ~/.codex/, ~/.config/codex/, ~/.local/share/codex/ ## Files to Modify - scripts/lib/doctor.sh: Update check_codex_auth() function ## Acceptance Criteria 1. No mention of OPENAI_API_KEY in codex auth check 2. Correctly detects OAuth authentication 3. Provides helpful guidance for unauthenticated users 4. Regenerate scripts/generated/doctor_checks.sh after changes","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T21:25:35.325519Z","updated_at":"2025-12-21T21:37:13.931458Z","closed_at":"2025-12-21T21:37:13.931458Z","close_reason":"Fixed check_codex_auth to detect OAuth tokens in ~/.codex/auth.json instead of checking OPENAI_API_KEY env var. Also cleaned up redundant shell redirects.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-epc","depends_on_id":"bd-ua5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T21:26:35.689670Z","created_by":"daemon","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-epc","depends_on_id":"bd-wy1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T21:26:37.126029Z","created_by":"daemon","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

@@ -150,6 +150,8 @@ export default function CompleteGuidePage() {

Before writing the plan itself, you need a coherent foundation. Think of it as assembling a foundation bundle: a tech stack decision, an initial architectural direction, a strong AGENTS.md file bootstrapped from a known-good template, up-to-date best-practices guides, and enough product and workflow explanation for the models to understand what "good" looks like.

+

Keep best practices guides in the project folder and reference them in AGENTS.md. These guides should be kept up to date; you can have Claude Code search the web and update them to the latest versions of your frameworks and libraries.

+

A strong bootstrap move is to start every new project by copying an AGENTS.md from an existing project that already has good general behavioral rules, safety notes, tool blurbs, and coordination guidance. Later, once the plan and beads are clearer, you ask agents to replace the project-specific content while preserving the general rules that carry across projects.

@@ -238,6 +240,8 @@ For each proposed change, give me your detailed analysis and rationale/justifica

Plans created this way routinely reach 3,000-6,000+ lines. They are not slop. They are the result of countless iterations and blending of ideas and feedback from many models. For the CASS GitHub Pages export feature, the plan went through multiple rounds over about 3 hours, growing to approximately 3,500 lines. You can also see a 6,000-line plan to get a feel for the scale.

It feels slow because no code is being written. But if you do it correctly and then start up enough agents in your swarm with Agent Mail, beads, and bv, the code will be written so ridiculously quickly that it more than makes up for this slow part. And what's more, the code will be really good.

+ +

When to stop refining and start converting to beads: Stay in plan refinement if whole-workflow questions are still moving around, major architecture debates are still open, or fresh models keep finding substantial missing features, constraints, or tradeoffs. Switch to beads when the plan mostly feels stable and the remaining improvements are about execution structure, testing obligations, sequencing, and embedded context rather than about what the system fundamentally is. If you are still redesigning the product, stay in plan space. If you are mainly packaging the work for execution, move to bead space.

 @@ -247,6 +251,10 @@ For each proposed change, give me your detailed analysis and rationale/justifica {/* SECTION 4: PLANS BECOME BEADS */} {/* ============================================================= */} + + Watch for the plan-bead gap. In the destructive_command_guard sessions, agents occasionally got stuck between plan revision and bead creation: the competing plan was revised but no beads were ever created. Always ensure the synthesis step concludes with an explicit transition to bead creation. + +

Then you're ready to turn the plan into beads. Think of these as epics, tasks, and subtasks with an associated dependency structure. The name comes from Steve Yegge's amazing project, which is like Jira or Linear, but optimized for use by coding agents. They are stored locally in .beads/ JSONL files that commit with your code.

There are two separate stages here. The planning is and should be prior to and orthogonal to beads. You should always have a super detailed markdown plan first. Then treat transforming that markdown plan into beads as a separate, distinct problem with its own challenges. But once you're in "bead space" you never look back at the markdown plan. But that's why it's so critical to transfer all the details over to the beads.

@@ -260,6 +268,10 @@ For each proposed change, give me your detailed analysis and rationale/justifica

For existing projects with a specific plan file, prefix it: "OK so now read ALL of PLAN_FILE_NAME.md; please take ALL of that and elaborate on it..." The rest of the prompt stays the same.

+ + Never write pseudo-beads in markdown documents. Go directly from the markdown plan to actual real beads using the br tool, and from that point on just add and change actual beads. If the model starts describing beads in text form instead of creating them, stop it and redirect to br create. + + @@ -548,6 +560,8 @@ bv --robot-triage --robot-triage-by-label # Group by domain`} /> +
I really think worktrees are a bad pattern and not worth the trouble. There are other, much better ways at getting multiple agents to work well together at the same time that don't come with the overhead and reconciliation burden of worktrees. I run like 10+ of them at once in a single project without git worktrees. Agent Mail is the solution.
+

All agents commit directly to main. This may surprise you if you're used to feature branches. But branch-per-agent creates merge hell with 10+ agents making frequent commits. Worktrees add filesystem complexity and path confusion. Agents lose context when switching branches. Logical conflicts survive textual merges: a function signature change on one branch and a new callsite on another merge cleanly but fail to compile. On a single branch, the second agent sees the signature change immediately and adapts.

Instead of branch isolation, three complementary mechanisms prevent conflicts: file reservations (agents reserve files via Agent Mail before editing; advisory, not rigid, with TTL expiry so dead agents cannot deadlock the system), a pre-commit guard (blocks commits to files reserved by another agent), and DCG (Destructive Command Guard, which mechanically blocks dangerous commands).

@@ -575,6 +589,8 @@ bv --robot-triage --robot-triage-by-label # Group by domain`} />

Every agent is a generalist. No role specialization. All agents read the same AGENTS.md and can pick up any bead. This is deliberately opposed to "specialist agent" architectures where one agent has a special role — specialist agents become single points of failure. When the specialist crashes or needs compaction, the whole system stalls. With 12 fungible agents, losing one makes almost no difference.

+

You also do not want "ringleaders": a coordinating boss agent whose crash takes down the whole system. Coordination must live in artifacts (beads, reservations, threads) and tools (bv, Agent Mail), not in any special agent.

+

Think of it like RaptorQ fountain codes: beads are "blobs" in a stream, any agent catches any bead in any order. There is no "rarest chunk" bottleneck, and the system is resilient to partial agent failures by design. Failure recovery is trivial: the bead remains marked in_progress, any other agent can resume it, and a replacement agent is just ntm add PROJECT --cc=1 plus the standard marching orders prompt.

When one agent breaks, it's not even a problem when all the agents are fungible. Agents become like commodities and can be instantiated and destroyed at will and the only downside is some slowdown and some wasted tokens.
@@ -659,6 +675,22 @@ When you're not sure what to do next, use the bv tool mentioned in AGENTS.md to whyItWorks="This is the closest thing to a canonical swarm kickoff packet. It front-loads the shared operating context, forces the agent to establish social presence through Agent Mail, and then pivots away from passive waiting toward execution. The line about 'communication purgatory' matters because swarm failure often comes from over-coordination rather than under-coordination. Under the hood, the prompt establishes a control loop: load rules, understand the codebase, join the coordination layer, claim work, keep state synchronized, and use bv whenever local judgment is insufficient. The rch requirement is especially important in real swarms because it externalizes expensive builds and tests, preventing local CPU contention from degrading the entire multi-agent system. That one sentence is operational, not cosmetic. The prompts are deliberately generic; their vagueness is a feature, letting you reuse them for every project while the agent gets specifics from AGENTS.md and the beads." /> + +

Newcomers often understand each individual tool but do not have a clean picture of the first live operating loop. In practice, the first 10 minutes look like this:

+ + Each agent uses bv --robot-triage and br ready --json to choose a bead., + "Before editing, the agent reserves the relevant file surface and announces the claim in the matching br-### thread.", + "Only then does the agent start coding, reviewing, or testing.", + ]} /> + +

That sequence turns a pile of terminals into a coordinated swarm. Skipping the join-up steps produces duplicate work, silent conflicts, and "communication purgatory." Skipping the routing steps means agents choose work randomly instead of unlocking the dependency graph intelligently.

+ + -

When you start up like 5 of each kind of agent and have them all collaborate in the same shared workspace, you can hit the classic "thundering herd" problem. The fix: stagger agent starts by 30 seconds minimum, make sure agents mark beads as in-progress quickly, and wait 4 seconds after launch before sending the initial prompt.

+

When you start up like 5 of each kind of agent and have them all collaborate in the same shared workspace, you can hit the classic "thundering herd" problem. The fix: stagger agent starts by 30 seconds minimum, make sure agents mark beads as in-progress quickly, and wait 4 seconds after launch before sending the initial prompt. For Codex specifically: send Enter twice after pasting long prompts (Codex has an input buffer quirk that sometimes swallows the first submit).

 @@ -710,10 +742,12 @@ When you're not sure what to do next, use the bv tool mentioned in AGENTS.md to
If you use the right tooling and workflows (agent mail + beads + bv), it transforms into 80% planning (AI-assisted, based on an initial prompt) and turning the markdown plan into very detailed and granular beads. And then the rest is just making sure the swarm of agents stay busy and executing their beads tasks effectively.
+

Taken to its endpoint, this design supports full autonomy: one puppet master agent controlling ntm via robot mode, replacing the human for routine machine-tending. The methodology is building toward a future where the human designs the plan, polishes the beads, and then walks away entirely while agents execute, review, ship, and start the next cycle.

+

When the "foregone conclusion" breaks down: If you find yourself doing heavy cognitive work during implementation, that is a signal that planning or bead polishing was insufficient. The remedies are specific: vague beads means agents improvise and produce inconsistent implementations; missing dependencies means agents work on tasks whose prerequisites are not done; thin AGENTS.md means agents produce non-idiomatic code; no Agent Mail means agents step on each other's files. The fix is always the same: pause implementation, go back to bead space, and add the missing detail.

- Watch for strategic drift. A swarm can look productive while heading in the wrong direction — agents generating lots of code and commits while the real goal still feels far away. If that happens, stop and ask: "Where are we on this project? Do we actually have the thing we are trying to build? If we intelligently implement all open beads, would we close that gap completely?" If the answer is no, add or revise beads, re-polish them, and resume with a corrected frontier. Busy agents are not the goal; a bead graph that actually converges on the project goal is the goal. + Watch for strategic drift. A swarm can look productive while heading in the wrong direction: agents generating lots of code and commits while the real goal still feels far away. A recent X post about FrankenEngine described these as "Come to Jesus" moments with the agents, used to make sure "we are not losing sight of the bigger picture" after days of methodically cranking through beads. If that happens, stop and ask: "Where are we on this project? Do we actually have the thing we are trying to build? If we intelligently implement all open beads, would we close that gap completely?" If the answer is no, add or revise beads, re-polish them, and resume with a corrected frontier. Busy agents are not the goal; a bead graph that actually converges on the project goal is the goal. @@ -763,7 +797,13 @@ caam activate claude backup-2 # Switch instantly`} /> whyItWorks="This prompt is short because it is not redirecting the agent into a new domain. It is forcing a mode switch from generative coding to adversarial reading. The phrase 'fresh eyes' pushes the model to reframe code it just wrote as something potentially wrong, confusing, or internally inconsistent. That reduces the pattern where an agent stops once code compiles and never performs the low-cost bug sweep that catches obvious issues. The most effective reviews use subagent delegation: dispatch a fresh subagent with no memory of the original implementation to review each changed file." /> -

Keep running rounds until they stop finding bugs. Typically 1-2 rounds for simple beads, 2-3 for complex ones. If an agent keeps finding bugs after 3 rounds, the implementation approach may be fundamentally off; consider having a different agent take over. Each review should answer four questions:

+

Keep running rounds until they stop finding bugs. Typically 1-2 rounds for simple beads, 2-3 for complex ones. If an agent keeps finding bugs after 3 rounds, the implementation approach may be fundamentally off; consider having a different agent take over.

+ + + You do not need to send the review prompt manually every time. Include it as part of the agent's workflow expectations in AGENTS.md: "After completing each bead, do a self-review before moving to the next one." Well-trained agents (with a good skill) will do this automatically. + + +

Each review should answer four questions:

Is the implementation correct? Does it do what the bead description says it should?, @@ -846,7 +886,11 @@ bun lint`} /> -

This phase is distinct from the per-bead self-reviews above. Self-reviews happen after each bead is completed and focus on the code that was just written. Deep review happens after all (or most) beads are done and casts a wider net across the entire codebase, looking for problems that only become visible when you see how all the pieces fit together. Every 30-60 minutes during active implementation, or after a natural milestone (e.g., all beads in an epic are done), trigger cross-agent review. Do not have all agents stop to review simultaneously; pick one or two agents that just finished a bead and send them the review prompt while the others keep implementing.

+

This phase is distinct from the per-bead self-reviews above. Self-reviews happen after each bead is completed and focus on the code that was just written. Deep review happens after all (or most) beads are done and casts a wider net across the entire codebase, looking for problems that only become visible when you see how all the pieces fit together.

+ +

Cross-agent review catches a fundamentally different class of bugs than self-review. When Agent A implements a function and Agent B calls it, Agent A's self-review will never catch the fact that Agent B is passing arguments in the wrong order, because Agent A does not know about Agent B's code. Cross-agent review surfaces these integration issues.

+ +

Every 30-60 minutes during active implementation, or after a natural milestone (e.g., all beads in an epic are done), trigger cross-agent review. Do not have all agents stop to review simultaneously; pick one or two agents that just finished a bead and send them the review prompt while the others keep implementing. This keeps the swarm productive while still catching inter-agent issues.

Keep doing rounds of these two prompts until they consistently come back clean with no changes made. These prompts serve different purposes and should be alternated. This is one of the more art-than-science parts of the methodology. The prompts overlap in literal meaning, but they reliably activate different search behaviors in the models:

~$500/month for Claude Max and GPT Pro subscriptions (at minimum), plus ~$50/month for a cloud server (OVH, Contabo). Multiple Max accounts may be needed for large swarms; CAAM enables instant switching when hitting rate limits. At scale, token usage for a single intensive session can reach ~20M input tokens and ~3.5M output tokens.

+

~$500/month for Claude Max and GPT Pro subscriptions (at minimum), plus ~$50/month for a cloud server (OVH, Contabo). Multiple Max accounts may be needed for large swarms; CAAM enables instant switching when hitting rate limits. At scale, token usage for a single intensive session can reach ~20M input tokens, ~3.5M output tokens, ~2.6M reasoning tokens, and ~1.15 billion cached token reads. At full scale: 22 Claude Max accounts, 22 GPT Pro accounts, and 7 Gemini Ultra accounts.

@@ -1473,6 +1524,8 @@ ntm spawn my-first-project --cc=2 --cod=1 --gmi=1`} />

You don't even need to know much at all about computers; you just need the desire to learn and some grit and determination. And about $500/month for the subscriptions, plus another $50 or so for the cloud server.

+

Once you get Claude Code up and running on the cloud server, you basically have an ultra competent friend who can help you with any other problems you encounter. And Jeffrey will personally answer your questions if you reach out on X or on GitHub issues.

+

If you want to change the entire direction of your life, it has truly never been easier. If you think you might want to do it, I really recommend just immersing yourself.

+

+        local success=false
+        local output=""
+        
+        if [[ "$DRY_RUN" == "true" ]]; then
+            log_item "skip" "Codex CLI" "dry-run"
+        else
+            for pkg in "@openai/codex@latest" "@openai/codex" "@openai/codex@$codex_fallback_version"; do
+                log_to_file "Trying bun install $pkg"
+                local attempt=1
+                local pkg_success=false
+                while [[ $attempt -le 3 ]]; do
+                    if output=$("$bun_bin" install -g --trust "$pkg" 2>&1); then
+                        pkg_success=true
+                        success=true
+                        break 2
+                    fi
+                    sleep 2
+                    attempt=$((attempt + 1))
+                done
+                log_to_file "Failed $pkg after 3 attempts: $output"
+            done
+            
+            if [[ "$success" == "true" ]]; then
+                if [[ "$QUIET" != "true" ]] && [[ "$VERBOSE" != "true" ]]; then
+                    printf "\033[1A\033[2K  ${GREEN}[ok]${NC} Codex CLI\n"
+                elif [[ "$QUIET" != "true" ]]; then
+                    printf "  ${GREEN}[ok]${NC} Codex CLI\n"
+                fi
+                ((SUCCESS_COUNT += 1))
+            else
+                if [[ "$QUIET" != "true" ]] && [[ "$VERBOSE" != "true" ]]; then
+                    printf "\033[1A\033[2K  ${RED}[FAIL]${NC} Codex CLI\n"
+                elif [[ "$QUIET" != "true" ]]; then
+                    printf "  ${RED}[FAIL]${NC} Codex CLI\n"
+                fi
+                log_to_file "All Codex install attempts failed. Last output: $output"
+                ((FAIL_COUNT += 1))
+            fi
         fi
 
         # Show version change without double-counting
